before: Calculate the perimeter of 2D shapes
Sketch 3D objects from different views
Visualise and sketch top, front and side views of 3D objects.
Work with nets of 3D objects
Find volume in cm³ and m³ from its dimensions.
Convert times between am/pm and 24-hour notation.
Convert flexibly between fraction, decimal (terminating/recurring) and percentage forms.
Convert rates such as km/h to m/s and vice versa.
Use root notation and the relationship between squares/cubes and their roots.
Convert between mm², cm², m², ha and km².
Convert between mm³, cm³, m³, mL, L, kL and ML.
Describe numerical data as discrete/continuous and categorical data as nominal/ordinal.
Explain how adding/removing values and outliers affect centre and range.
Add, subtract, multiply and divide algebraic fractions with pronumerals and/or indices in the denominator.
Recognise polynomial expressions and describe degree, leading term/coefficient, constant term and monic/non-monic.
Apply Pythagoras' theorem in 3D
Find edge and diagonal lengths of rectangular prisms and other 3D objects.
Apply trigonometry in 3D
Solve right-angled triangle problems in 3 dimensions, including bearings and elevation/depression.
Prove properties of isosceles/equilateral triangles and special quadrilaterals from their definitions.
Prove and use tangent and secant properties, including the tangent–chord angle and intersecting chords/secants.
Distinguish mutually exclusive from non-mutually exclusive events and describe them using inclusive/exclusive or.
Use stopping distance = reaction distance + braking distance.
Distance and midpoint in 3D
Use P∧Q (and), P∨Q (or), ¬P (not) and implication and equivalence notation (⇒, ⇔).
Prove inequalities using the definition that a>b if and only if a−b>0.
Prove inequalities using the fact that squares of real numbers are non-negative, in particular (a±b)²≥0.
Prove and use order results such as adding the same number, reciprocals of positives, |a|>|b|⇒a²>b², transitivity, and adding or multiplying inequalities.
Prove and use the triangle inequality |a+b|≤|a|+|b| and interpret it geometrically.
Establish and use the relationship (a+b)/2≥√(ab) between the arithmetic and geometric means for two non-negative numbers.
Prove results using the squeeze theorem: if f(x)≤g(x)≤h(x) near k and f and h share a limit L, then g→L.
Define the direction vector of a line and identify PQ⃗ as a direction vector of the line through P and Q in 2D and 3D.
Establish the relationship between the gradient m of a line in 2D and its direction vector.
Use r=a+λb as the vector equation of a line in 2D and 3D to solve problems.
Use r=p+λ(q−p) for the line through P and Q and interpret the value of λ as position along the line.
Convert a line in 2D between gradient–intercept form y=mx+c and vector form.
Determine when two lines given in vector form are parallel in 2D and 3D.
Determine whether three points are collinear in 2D and 3D using vector methods.
Intersection of lines in 2D
Find the point of intersection of two non-parallel lines expressed as vector equations in 2D.
Intersection of lines in 3D
Determine whether two lines in 3D intersect and, if so, find the parameter value and coordinates of intersection.
Define skew lines in 3D and apply vector methods to determine whether two lines are skew.
Express a parametrically defined curve in 2D or 3D as a vector equation r=(x(t),y(t)) or (x(t),y(t),z(t)).
Recognise r=rcosθ i+rsinθ j and |r−c|=r as vector equations of a circle in 2D.
Recognise |r−c|=r as the vector equation of a sphere in 3D with centre C and radius r.
Determine the Cartesian equation of a curve in 2D from its vector equation and graph the curve.
Establish and use i·i=j·j=k·k=1 and i·j=j·k=k·i=0.
Prove and use the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality |a·b|≤|a||b| for vectors.
Solve problems and prove geometric results in 2D and 3D using vectors.
Define the number i by i²=−1.
Solve x²+k=0
Use the number i to solve quadratic equations of the form x²+k=0 where k is a positive real number.
Identify the real part Re(z) and imaginary part Im(z) of a complex number z=a+ib.
Use the condition that z₁=z₂ if and only if their real parts are equal and their imaginary parts are equal.
Define the complex conjugate z̅=a−ib of z=a+ib and use it to solve problems.
Define and calculate the modulus |z|=√(zz̅)=√(a²+b²).
Establish and use Re(z)=(z+z̅)/2, Im(z)=(z−z̅)/(2i) and 1/z=z̅/|z|².
Find the two square roots of a complex number z=a+ib.
Plot a complex number z=a+ib as a point on the complex (Argand) plane.
Define and calculate the argument θ of a non-zero complex number, noting it has multiple values differing by multiples of 2π.
Define and use the principal argument Arg(z), the unique argument in the interval (−π,π].
Express a complex number in modulus–argument form z=r(cosθ+isinθ) and represent it on the complex plane.
Prove and use |z₁z₂|=|z₁||z₂|, |z₁/z₂|=|z₁|/|z₂| and |zⁿ|=|z|ⁿ for integer n.
Prove and use arg(z₁z₂)=arg z₁+arg z₂, arg(z₁/z₂)=arg z₁−arg z₂ and arg(zⁿ)=n·arg z.
Prove and use the triangle inequality |z₁+z₂|≤|z₁|+|z₂| for complex numbers.
Solve quadratic equations ax²+bx+c=0 where a, b and c are complex numbers.
Prove de Moivre’s theorem (cosθ+isinθ)ⁿ=cos nθ+isin nθ for positive integer n using mathematical induction.
nth roots of ±1
Determine the nth roots of ±1 in polar form and their location on the unit circle.
Geometry of the nth roots of ±1
Illustrate the geometric relationship connecting the nth roots of ±1.
Graph Re(z)=c and Im(z)=k
Graph vertical and horizontal lines of the form Re(z)=c or Im(z)=k.
Graph |z−z₁|=|z−z₂|
Graph the line |z−z₁|=|z−z₂| and give a geometrical description of it.
Graph |z|=r and |z−z₁|=r
Graph the circles |z|=r and |z−z₁|=r.
Graph rays arg(z)=θ
Graph rays corresponding to arg(z)=θ and arg(z−z₁)=θ.
Use product-to-sum identities to evaluate integrals of the form ∫sin(mx)cos(nx)dx, ∫sin(mx)sin(nx)dx or ∫cos(mx)cos(nx)dx.
Derive sinA=2t/(1+t²), cosA=(1−t²)/(1+t²) and tanA=2t/(1−t²) where t=tan(A/2).
Derive the expressions for acceleration dv/dt, v(dv/dx) and d/dx(½v²).
Use F=m¨x
Find acceleration ¨x using F=m¨x, where F is the force acting on a mass m.
Recognise forces as vectors, resolve concurrent forces into perpendicular components and use projections to find the force in a given direction in 2D and 3D.
Define simple harmonic motion as ¨x=−n²(x−c), where acceleration is proportional to and opposite in direction to displacement from the centre.
Verify that x=Asin(nt+α)+c, x=Acos(nt+α)+c and combinations of these satisfy the SHM differential equation.
Prove that motion is simple harmonic by obtaining ¨x=−n²(x−c) from a given equation for acceleration, velocity or displacement.
Derive v²=n²[A²−(x−c)²] for a particle in simple harmonic motion, where A is the amplitude.
Derive, from ΣF=m¨x, the acceleration equation for a particle resisted by a force proportional to a power of the speed.
2D shapes: Classify two-dimensional shapes and describe their properties
2D shapes: Dissect two-dimensional shapes and rearrange them using translations, reflections and rotations
3D objects: Compare, describe and name prisms and pyramids
3D objects: Connect three-dimensional objects with two-dimensional representations
3D objects: Construct prisms and pyramids
Examine the gradient/slope-intercept form
Apply formulas to find the midpoint and gradient/slope of an interval on the Cartesian plane
Examine and describe a graph/network
Trigonometry in three dimensions: Interpret 3D contexts and apply trigonometry to solve problems in three dimensions
Introduction to 2D and 3D vectors: Represent points and vectors in 2D and 3D in component, ordered-pair/triple and column form, and use the distance and midpoint formulas
Vector equations of lines and curves: Use r=a+λb for lines in 2D and 3D, test parallelism, collinearity, intersection and skewness, and express curves, circles and spheres as vector equations
Powers and roots of complex numbers: Prove and use de Moivre’s theorem, find nth roots of ±1 and of complex numbers, and use complex numbers as vectors
The t-formulas: Derive sinA, cosA and tanA in terms of t=tan(A/2) and use them to solve trigonometric equations
Forces and further motion in a straight line: Use the acceleration expressions dv/dt, v(dv/dx) and d/dx(½v²), apply Newton’s laws and F=m­¨x, and resolve forces into components
Simple harmonic motion: Define SHM by ¨x=−n²(x−c), verify solutions, derive v²=n²[A²−(x−c)²], and graph and solve SHM problems
Classifying 2D shapes and the area of rectangles.
Building 3D objects and the volume of rectangular prisms.
Gradient–intercept form and parallel/perpendicular lines.
3D trigonometry and the sine, cosine and area rules.
2D and 3D vectors, the dot product and projections, and motion and projectiles in vector form.
